data.title: Applied Digital: Powering The AI Boom
data.description:
The Backbone of AI Infrastructure

Applied Digital Corporation (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:APLD) has delivered a set of results that have not gone unnoticed, and after the rally the stock has experienced, I believe it’s worth shedding some light on its business model, especially if you’re still trying to map out where it fits within the broader AI infrastructure landscape.

The company’s model revolves around building and managing the physical infrastructure used by firms specializing in high-performance computing (HPC). These clients deploy their own GPUs within Applied Digital’s facilities to provide GPU cloud and HPC services to third parties.

Currently, the company operates two campuses in North Dakota, Jamestown and Ellendale, with 100 MW and 180 MW of capacity, respectively, and another in Garden City, Texas, with 200 MW. Virtually all this capacity is already contracted, and the company plans to expand its installed capacity to more than 1 GW by 2027 through its new project in Harwood (ND), which will add over 500 MW.

If you’re not familiar with the scale of these figures, it helps to compare: companies like Equinix (EQIX) (though operating under a different business model) manage networks exceeding +2 GW worldwide. In contrast, APLD already concentrates 0.5 GW across just three U.S. sites, and reaching 1 GW by 2027 would position the company directly within the growth engine of AI computing.

And there’s no better proof of that than the CoreWeave case. Applied Digital has signed 400 MW worth of lease agreements with CoreWeave across three phases through 2027. These contracts represent more than $10B in guaranteed payments through 2035, with revenue expected to grow from around $80M in 2026 to more than $600M by 2029, once all capacity is fully operational.

From Blockchain Hosting to High-Performance Computing

After reviewing the company’s quarterly results, APLD reported $64.2M in revenue, up 84% year-over-year. However, these figures require some interpretation, since nearly 41% of that total came from tenant fit-out work performed for CoreWeave.

Specifically, the $26.3M in fit-out revenue represents a one-time item related to the build-out of CoreWeave’s first 100 MW building within the Ellendale (Polaris Forge 1) campus. Once this phase is completed, it will transition into recurring lease revenue within the company’s HPC vertical, but for this quarter, it was recognized as part of the construction process.

The same logic applies to expenses. While the company reported $55.6M in cost of revenue, roughly $25M of that total was directly tied to the fit-out project, offset by the related revenue and leaving an effective gross margin of around 5%.

It’s also worth pausing on the $16.6M in stock-based compensation (SBC). APLD recognized the remaining unvested portion of certain incentive plans in a single quarter, but this did not involve any cash outflow. Essentially, the shift from its former blockchain hosting model to its new HPC infrastructure business led management to establish updated incentive programs tied to upcoming performance milestones.

In my opinion, if both the fit-out effect and the SBC expense are excluded, operating expenses would have remained in line with prior quarters. roughly $12M, indicating that cost discipline remains intact.

After these adjustments, the underlying hosting business shows an operating margin between 10% and 15%, well above the negative GAAP margin reported this quarter. In fact, adjusted EBITDA came in positive at $0.5M, which tells me the core business is beginning to sustain itself even before the lease revenues from the CoreWeave agreement start flowing through. The reported loss per share of –$0.11, therefore, reflects accounting timing rather than operational deterioration.

Bridging the Gap Between Market Price and Contracted Value

With the quarterly results now clarified, we can move on to assigning a valuation for APLD’s stock. At the time of writing, the company has a market capitalization of $9.5B and net debt of around $670M, resulting in an enterprise value of roughly $10.2B. In my view, as discussed earlier, the most logical approach to valuing the company is based on EV per contracted MW, which better reflects the economics of its HPC model.

At present, the market is valuing each operational MW at approximately $21M, based on its current EV divided by installed capacity. Meanwhile, the CoreWeave contracts secure about $28M per MW over the next 15 years, roughly $11B in guaranteed payments. This difference suggests to me that the stock still trades below the value implied by its contracted cash flows, representing a discount to the intrinsic worth of its HPC infrastructure.

That said, it’s also useful to compare APLD using the EV/Sales multiple against peers such as EQIX and Digital Realty (DLR). While these companies operate on a global scale, they share a similar underlying data center business structure, making this a valid framework for projection. Based on current projections, by 2028 APLD is expected to reach 1 GW of installed capacity and around $880 million in annual revenue, implying a forward EV/Sales multiple of about 11.6x.

In other words, APLD’s valuation would compress from its current 34x EV/Sales to roughly 11.6x by FY28 as HPC contract revenues begin to consolidate. In my opinion, this multiple is defensible when compared with the profitability and stability of Equinix and Digital Realty. Both maintain EBITDA margins above 40% with steady cash flows, whereas APLD remains in its buildout phase, with a gross margin around 22% and barely positive EBITDA.

To me, these future cash flows are what the market is already pricing in to justify today’s valuation, making an EV/Sales of roughly 12x by FY28 a reasonable assumption.

Under this scenario, with expected revenue of $880M and a 12x multiple, APLD’s enterprise value would reach approximately $10.5B. Dividing this by 279M shares outstanding yields a price per share slightly above $37.

That would be my base valuation with the data currently available. However, there’s additional upside potential. According to the latest earnings call, APLD has an active pipeline of about 4 GW and is in negotiations with two hyperscalers that could add another 400–500 MW before FY28. If any of these agreements materialize, revenue could exceed $1.2B, making a valuation in the $45–$50 range justifiable.

In my opinion, given the company’s current momentum, a price target between $40 and $45 per share reflects the fundamental value of what APLD could achieve within three years, though at those levels, the margin of safety would narrow. If the rally toward that range is driven by new contracts or major announcements, I would recommend re-evaluating whether it’s worth taking partial profits or maintaining the position for further upside.

What Could Break the Bull Case

While the scenario described earlier represents the ideal outcome, and despite the solidity of APLD’s signed contracts, there are also factors that could justify the current price levels as potentially overextended. For example, with just $114M in cash at the end of the quarter, the company’s liquidity today depends largely on the structured credit facilities provided by Macquarie. If any of these tranches were delayed by more than 90 days or if the construction costs for Polaris Forge exceeded current estimates by 10% or more, operating cash flow could come under pressure, potentially forcing the company to issue new equity to fund ongoing projects.

Ironically, CoreWeave itself represents another risk factor. Assuming the company reaches 1 GW of capacity within the next three years, roughly 80% of total revenue would still be tied to a single customer. Although the contract provides 15 years of visibility and predictable cash flows, any credit-related event or potential price renegotiation could materially impact cash generation.

Finally, one cannot ignore the possibility of a sentiment shift within the broader AI narrative. A slowdown in AI infrastructure demand could trigger multiple compression, with APLD’s EV/Sales contracting from around 12x to 8x, should large-scale projects be postponed or the industry face supply-chain or energy bottlenecks. In that scenario, a combination of liquidity strain, construction delays, or dependency risk on CoreWeave could bring the stock back toward the $20 range, without necessarily invalidating the company’s long-term bullish trend.

A Long-Term Buy on Execution and Scale

With all the data on the table, my reading is that Applied Digital is now at the inflection point where its business model becomes tangible. The CoreWeave agreement not only validates its transition into the HPC infrastructure space but also provides a long-term revenue stream that, if executed according to schedule, could sustain roughly 40% annualized growth over the next three years.

At current levels, I believe the market is already pricing in a significant portion of these future cash flows tied to CoreWeave, as reflected in an implied EV/Sales of around 12x based on FY28 projections. Still, the company’s 4 GW active pipeline and the stability of 15-year take-or-pay contracts make a long-term bullish stance on APLD fundamentally justified.

That said, execution will be key. Any material delay in energizing new capacity or a cooling in AI infrastructure demand could compress the multiple and send the stock temporarily below $20. My base case, however, supports a Buy rating on APLD, with a 36-month price target between $40 and $45 per share.

At those levels, the risk-reward balance would narrow, making it a logical point to consider partial or full profit-taking, unless new contracts or expansion milestones justify revisiting the upside scenario.

data.title: Capital One: Strong Quarter And Much Value To Be Extracted From Discover Integration
data.description:
Capital One Financial Corporation (NYSE:COF) has made a transformative acquisition by acquiring Discover Financial Services, which is expected to strengthen its position in credit cards and lead to significant synergies over the coming years. As this is my first coverage on Capital One, in this article I will do an overview of its business profile and discuss what the Discover acquisition means for its business, followed by an analysis of its financial profile and valuation.

Business Overview

Capital One is a financial holding company, operating in the consumer credit segment of the financial services industry. At the end of last September, it had close to $662 billion in total assets, being a mid-sized company in the financial sector by this measure. Its current market value is about $138 billion, and its shares trade in the New York Stock Exchange.

The company offers several banking and financial products and services to consumers, small businesses, and commercial clients, through its own retail network, digital channels, affiliate marketing, and strategic partnerships.

Capital One is one of the largest credit cards issuers in the U.S. based on the outstanding balance of credit card loans, with its most important competitors including JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Citigroup (C), and American Express (AXP). While credit cards are the company’s most important product, it also offers other banking products, such as deposits, auto loans, treasury management, bank lending, beyond others. Geographically, the domestic market is the most important one by far, but Capital One also operates internationally, namely in Canada and the United Kingdom.

At the end of last September, Capital One’s loan book amounted to $443 billion, being fully funded by customer deposits of $468 billion. This means its loan-to-deposit ratio was about 95% at the end of last quarter, being an acceptable ratio compared to other banks. This also means that Capital One is not much reliant on wholesale financing to fund its loan book, being a positive feature for the sustainability of its business model over the long term.

Regarding its growth strategy, Capital One has been focused on the expansion of its credit cards segment and has also invested in digital transformation to better serve its customers and improve operating efficiency. Indeed, Capital One has fully migrated its operating platforms to the cloud, being one of the few U.S. banks to do that, aiming to be at the forefront of the digital transformation in banking industry.

Beyond its organic growth initiatives, Capital One has also seek external growth, of which the recent $35 billion acquisition of Discover Financial Services is the most important one.

This acquisition was announced back in February 2024, and closed last May, boosting Capital One’s position in the credit cards segment in a significant way. The merger of the two companies was friendly through an all-stock transaction, in which Discover’s shareholders received 1.092 shares of Capital One for each share they owned.

Discover had about $121 billion in loans at the end of 2024 and reported a net profit of $1.2 billion during the last year, having a smaller size than Capital One. Discover’s business is like Capital One, being largely exposed to credit cards which represented about 84% of its loan book, making a good fit from a business perspective with Capital One.

While this merger boosted Capital One’s size in credit cards and there are certainly significant cost savings in this segment to be achieved by combining the two companies, the most attractive feature of this acquisition is, in my opinion, Discover’s own payments network.

Capital One is one of the largest issuers of Visa (V) and MasterCard (MA) credit cards, but it didn’t had its own payment network. With this acquisition, Capital One is now more vertically integrated controlling both the car issuance and transaction processing, making its business model similar to American Express and Discover as a stand-alone company.

Capital One has now a proprietary payment network through Discover, which is present across the globe and accepted at more than 70 million merchant locations worldwide, being a transformative deal for Capital One. This allows the company to compete directly with American Express, Visa, and MasterCard on a global basis, aiming to have a globally competitive payments platform that will support its growth over the long term.

The merger is also quite interesting from a financial point of view given that Capital One can generate significant cost and revenues synergies, through the adoption of its cloud-native platform for Discover operations, expanding customer base and cross-selling opportunities, plus cost savings from using its own payment network instead of just relying on third-party providers. Not surprisingly, Capital One expects to reach around $2.5 billion synergies by 2027, showing that the financial impact of this merger is quite significant and adds to the strategic rationale of the merger.

Going forward, Capital One should be focused on the integration of Discover over the next few years, which means large acquisitions aren’t much likely in the near future. However, its growth strategy is not expected to change much over the long term, being a mix of organic initiatives and potential acquisitions if the opportunity arises.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, Capital One has a positive track record as a stand-alone company over the past few years, benefiting from the positive economic backdrop in the U.S. and very low levels of unemployment in recent years. Indeed, as shown in the next graph, the unemployment rate has remained at very low levels over the past decade in the U.S., with the only exception being during Covid when it spiked temporarily to more than 12%.

Over the past few years, the unemployment rate declined to around 3.5% during 2022-23, having gradually increased over the past eighteen months to a current level around 4.2%. This is important because there is a strong correlation between employment levels and credit quality, especially in the consumer segment. Given that Capital One is largely exposed to credit cards and consumer loans, the resilient U.S. economy and relatively low levels of unemployment have been strong supports for a positive financial performance in the recent past.

More recently, its financial figures were significantly impacted by the consolidation of Discover, with Q3 2025 being the first reported full quarter for the combined companies.

This explains why Capital One’s total assets increased to more than $661 billion at the end of last September, up by 36% YoY. Its loan book increased by 38% YoY to $443 billion, while deposits were up by 33% to $468 billion. This shows that the integration of Discover had a significant impact on Capital One’s size, having now about 19% of the U.S. credit cards market, being the largest player following the consolidation of Discover, according to Card Rates data.

From a credit quality perspective, Discover has a quality portfolio and better metrics than Capital One, given that it had a lower net charge-offs (NCO) ratio of 4.64% in 2024, compared to Capital One’s NCO rate of 4.96% last year. Despite that, Capital One made an initial $8.8 billion credit allowance in Q2 2025 when integrated Discover, under the current expected credit loss (CECL) model. As the company integrates the business and is more aware of its underlying risks, it’s likely that it will do allowance reversals if credit performance remains good. Effectively, it has made an allowance reversal of $760 million in Q3 2025, having a total allowance for credit losses of $23.1 billion at the end of the quarter, which the company sees as comfortable for the credit risk of the portfolio.

Regarding revenues, its net interest income (NII) increased by 54% YoY to $12.4 billion in Q3 2025, driven mainly by the Discover integration, and total revenues amounted to more than $15 billion in the quarter. Its net interest margin was 8.36%, much higher than in the previous quarters, due to Discover and lower rates on deposits.

Its operating costs increased by 55% YoY to nearly $8.3 billion, leading to a cost-to-income ratio of 55% in the quarter. While this is an acceptable efficiency ratio, the company has plenty of room for improvement ahead, as synergies from the merger are expected to ramp up in the fourth quarter and during 2026. Indeed, according to analysts’ estimates, its efficiency ratio is expected to improve gradually over the next few years, to a level of about 49% by 2027, showing that synergies from Discover acquisition should be an important growth driver for earnings over the next couple of years.

Regarding its bottom-line, it was close to $3.1 billion in Q3 2025, up by 82% YoY, but investors should note that this was driven largely by the Discover acquisition. Indeed, adjusted for Discover, Capital One’s purchase volume growth was 6.5% YoY in the last quarter, and its loan book increased by 3.5% YoY, showing that its underlying growth was solid but much more modest.

In the near term, the company continues to see good growth opportunities from the Discover integration, which should bode well for loan and revenue growth over the coming quarters, while credit quality has been trending positively in recent quarters and Capital One is not expecting this trend to change much in the near term.

On the capital front, the company’s recent earnings generation and the merger with Discover have been positive for its CET1 ratio, being at 14.4% at the end of Q3 2025. Capital One wants to operate near a CET1 ratio of 11% in the future, being a level that the company seems to be comfortable with over the long term.

This means Capital One has an excess capital position, even though I think a CET1 ratio around 11% seems somewhat low for a company that is largely exposed to credit cards and consumer loans. Compared to its closest peers, American Express has a CET1 ratio of 10.5%, but its has much better credit quality due to a lower NCO ratio, while Synchrony Financial (SYF) CET1 ratio is 13.7%. I agree that Capital One doesn’t need to have a 14.4% CET1 ratio and can easily operate at a lower level but would prefer to see a CET1 ratio in the 12-13% range over the long term.

Having said that, Capital One clearly has a strong financial position allowing it to return a good part of its excess capital and annual earnings to shareholders. Not surprisingly, the company announced a new $16 billion share buyback program, replacing its previous authorization, and has decided to increase its quarterly dividend by 33% to $0.80 per share. This shows that Capital One is clearly committed to returning excess capital to shareholders, being a support for its share price ahead. However, at its current share price, Capital One offers a forward-dividend yield of about 1.4%, not being much attractive for income-oriented investors.

Regarding its valuation, Capital One is currently trading at 1.28x book value and 11.5x forward earnings, which seem to be somewhat undemanding for a company that is likely to extract significant synergies from the acquisition of Discover and report higher profitability levels in the future. Furthermore, this is also an attractive valuation compared to its peers, especially compared to American Express that operate at much higher multiples. Considering that Capital One has now a business model more comparable to American Express, and Discover also traded at higher multiples in the past (between 1.6-2.2 book value) than Capital One, I think it’s quite possible that Capital One will re-rate to higher valuation multiples over the next couple of years.

Conclusion

Capital One has delivered a good quarterly performance and the integration of Discover is expected to be a strong support for earnings growth ahead, as the deal synergies are quite significant especially from the move of the debit business to the Discover network.

Moreover, from a capital perspective the company has an excess position, allowing it to return significant capital to shareholders, boding well for a higher valuation in the near future. On the other hand, investors should be aware that Capital One is heavily exposed to consumer credit and the macroeconomic environment is not great right now, raising some questions about credit quality ahead and its potential negative impact on earnings over the next few quarters.

While I think Capital One is a good play in the financial sector over the next couple of years, there are risks from a potential economic slowdown ahead that investors shouldn’t overlook.

data.title: Constellation Brands: I'm In With Buffett
data.description:
Recently, I've started to accumulate a new position in Constellation Brands (STZ). In this article, I'll outline my general investment thesis and offer supporting data.

One factor in my decision: Warren Buffett, arguably the greatest investor of our generation, has taken a $2 billion stake in the company via Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B)

Constellation Brands is a Consumer Staples stock. The company generates 84 percent of its revenue from sales of premium imported beer; specifically, via the Modelo, Corona, and Pacifico labels. The balance of the company's revenue come from wine and spirits sales.

Investment Thesis

Here's a bullet point investment thesis:

  • Constellation Brands is well-positioned to create long-term shareholder value despite short-term headwinds.

  • The operation revolves around a high-margin, cash-rich business model.

  • The balance sheet is sound.

  • The stock is significantly undervalued.

  • I like having Warren Buffett / Berkshire Hathaway as a fellow stockholder.

Let's break it down further.

Constellation Brands Positioned to Succeed

In order to out-compete peers, there are three basic strategies.

  • Be the low-cost provider

  • Offer differentiated products / services

  • Niche player

Key Point 1: Constellation Brands offers customers differentiated products: premium imported beers and generally higher-end wines and spirits. The company does not compete on low price. One may argue Constellation Brands is also a niche player: the beer segment clearly focuses upon Hispanic clientele. Its beer is brewed in Mexico and imported to the United States.

What's the acid test for a differentiated business strategy: a level of pricing power even in down markets!

Through the first 6 months of the 2026 fiscal year (ended August 31), Constellation Brands beer segment net sales per shipment increased about 1.6 percent. Yet shipments declined by ~6 percent and depletion eased by 3 percent. Depletion is defined as U.S. distributor to retail customers.

Therefore, through the first half fiscal 2026, beer shipments were down, but revenue per shipment was up. Hence, the beer segment demonstrated a level of pricing power.

Key Point 2: The company has a solid pipeline of new products: including new flavors, non-alcoholic beer, and lower-calorie brews. These products target younger consumers.

Key Point 3: Capital expenditures are expected to ease in coming quarters, as brewery expansion projects near completion. In turn, this should result in greater free cash flow.

TTM Feb2025 Feb2024 Feb2023 Feb2022

Through fiscal 2Q 2026, Constellation Brands enjoys a 21.6 percent free cash flow margin. That's a fine margin for any business. Generally, FCF margins between 10 percent and 20 percent are considered strong.

Key Point 4: U.S. beer and alcohol sales are expected to continue to decline, though unlikely as steep a trajectory as tobacco sales. However, tobacco companies adjusted and retained viability. For example, Altria (MO) stock has risen 68 percent in five years (excluding a robust dividend) despite nil net sales growth. The premium beer business has the capability to follow the same playbook.

I believe STZ management recognizes the macro and short-term challenges facing the business. They plan on taking the necessary steps to shore up the company.

  • Management is not reducing advertising spend; to the contrary, they intend to increase it. Incremental advertising is planned to focus upon non-Hispanic consumers and away-from-home sales (meaning bars and restaurants).

  • Opex and capex will be trimmed; seeking to retain or improve margins and run for cash flow.

  • Non-core, lower-margin wine and spirits business lines have been jettisoned.

  • The company is re-working packaging design to maximize product volumes per unit while lowering packaging costs.

The following presentation slide from the most recent earnings release captures much of the foregoing discussion.

High Margin / Cash Rich Business Model

Current challenges notwithstanding, Constellation Brands remains a high-margin, cash-rich business.

Here's peer-comparative margin figures:

On balance, Constellation Brands enjoys some of the best margins in the business.

Next, let's examine cash flow, free cash flow, and net income:

All three peer companies earn their profits in cash: operating cash flow is greater than net income. Since over the next several quarters Constellation Brands' capital expenditures are forecast to ease, investors may expect even greater STZ cash flow / profit spreads.

Meanwhile, the 2.9 percent dividend yield is well-covered by free cash flow. The TTM coverage ratio is a comfortable 39 percent. The dividend has been increased for 9 consecutive years. Over the past five years, the dividend has increased by about 7 percent a year.

Strong Balance Sheet and Debt Coverage

By standard measures, Constellation Brands' balance sheet is sound.

The current ratio is 1.08x. A CR above 1.0x is considered adequate.

Long-term debt is trending down. Fiscal YE2023 long-term debt was $11.3 billion. For the period ending August 31, 2025, long-term debt was $9.8 billion.

Long-term debt to equity is 136 percent as compared to the Beverages Industry average of 148 percent.

TTM interest coverage ratio (EBIT / net interest income) is 8.2x. A coverage ratio above 3x is considered adequate.

Constellation Brands Is Undervalued

STZ is undervalued by general valuation metrics. A simple reversion to the long-term mean suggests material investment upside.

Let's highlight valuation by using two FAST graphs.

First, here's a long-term chart illustrating price-to-operating earnings.

Constellation Brands long-term P/E is about 20x. Using management's $11.45 midpoint FY2026 EPS guidance and a conservative 15x multiple indicates a $172 stock.

Next, here's a similar graph charting price-to-operating cash flow.

The long-term P/CF multiple is 15x. Once again using management's $14.48 midpoint FY2026 OCF guidance and reducing the valuation multiple to 12x, the resulting Fair Value is $174.

It's important to recognize that these FVEs utilize current management guidance and conservative valuation multiples, several turns below the long-term averages. Furthermore, the charts indicate Street estimates for FY2027 earnings and cash flow are expected to rebound.

Nonetheless, a $173 Fair Value bid on STZ shares suggests ~20 percent upside, excluding the 2.9 percent dividend yield.

Indeed, a major part of my investment thesis rests upon conservative valuation premises. Such valuation is predicated upon using “trough year” management midpoint guidance, presuming no financial out-performance, and ignoring the potential for valuation multiple expansion if senior leadership is able to navigate the business to resume modest earnings and cash flow growth in FY2027 and beyond.

Fellow Shareholder Is Berkshire Hathaway

As outlined in the investment thesis bullet points, I place weight upon having Warren Buffett and Berkshire Hathaway as a fellow shareholder.

Unquestionably, current market sentiment for STZ stock is low. The Seeking Alpha quant rating is 1.58, equating to a Sell rating. Fidelity Investments give the stock a 1.1 rating, also indicating a Sell rating.

A combination of low Street ratings, sentiment, and valuation multiples: yet Berkshire Hathaway accumulating STZ common stock since 4Q 2024 point towards a defensible contrarian stock pick.

To be fair, other reputable Street analysts, namely CFRA and Morningstar, are constructive on Constellation Brands common shares.

Risks To The Thesis

My Constellation Brands investment thesis includes several identifiable investment risks.

  • Macro factors may become worse than expected: particularly an accelerated downward trend of reduced U.S. alcohol consumption.

  • Management's innovation / new products pipeline may not prove to be successful. Other premium beer competitors may still out-compete Constellation Brands.

  • Hispanic customers' belt-tightening on beer may not be a short-term phenomenon.

  • U.S. / Mexico tariff pressure on Constellation Brands Mexican breweries may not dissipate quickly: in FY2026, tariff headwinds are expected to be in the range of $90 million; or about $0.50 a share.

Concluding Thoughts

Constellation Brands represents a beaten-down, undervalued and unloved stock. Macro and industry-specific economic issues present management challenges. However, this is precisely why I like this investment.

I contend management is capable of navigating the company successfully through the current challenges; foremost, recent management commentary indicates they clearly recognize the problems.

The company is build upon a sound balance sheet, consistently high margins, strong cash flows, and declining capital expenditures.

Investor sentiment and valuation is simply too low to ignore. When you're face down in the gutter, just rolling over is a big improvement.

I believe the stock offers a conservative ~20 percent capital appreciation upside based upon “trough year” management guidance and understated valuation multiples. If the business can be stabilized and return to normalized growth and valuation multiples, STZ represents a potentially excellent opportunity for patient investors.

Please do your own careful due diligence before making any investment decision. This article is not a recommendation to buy or sell any stock. Good luck with all your 2025 investments.

data.title: Allete: Opportunity For One Last Bite Of The Cherry
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Background

In previous articles on Allete, Inc. (NYSE:ALE), I have explained how superior returns might be achieved buying at the then current share price and waiting for the $67 payment promised on completion of the Merger first announced on May 5, 2024, per the filing ("the Merger").

Key issues have been if and when the Merger, which is subject to shareholder and various regulatory approvals, might close. The Minnesota Public Utilities Commission ("MPUC") has now agreed to the Merger of Allete, Inc. per the excerpt below from an October 3, 2025 press release by Allete:

ALLETE, Inc. (NYSE: ALE) (the Company) today announced that the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission (MPUC) voted unanimously to approve its acquisition by Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPP Investments) and Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP). With all required regulatory approvals secured, the transaction is expected to close in late 2025 following issuance of the MPUC’s written order.

Subsequent to the May 5, 2024 Merger announcement, on October 1, 2024, BlackRock, Inc. (NYSE: BLK) announced the successful completion of its acquisition of Global Infrastructure Partners (“GIP”).

Common Stock Shareholders' Dividend Entitlement

In regard to quarterly dividends, the Merger agreement (linked above) prohibits payments of dividends with exceptions per following excerpts:

('A') quarterly cash dividends payable by the Company in respect of shares of Company Common Stock with declaration, record, increase and payment dates consistent with the Company’s past practices in an amount per share of Company Common Stock not in excess of five percent (5%) per share more than the dividend payable during the prior 12-month period,... and ('D') a “stub period” dividend to holders of record of Company Common Stock as of immediately prior to the Effective Time equal to the product of (1) the number of days from the record date for payment of the last quarterly dividend paid by the Company prior to the Effective Time, multiplied by (2) a daily dividend rate determined by dividing the amount of the last quarterly dividend paid prior to the Effective Time by ninety-one (91).

Accordingly, a further quarterly dividend payment can be expected by common stock shareholders ("the Shareholders"), before or at the time of payment of the $67 per share Merger acquisition price. There was an annual dividend increase of $0.025 per share per quarter in the first quarter of 2025 and increases of $0.0275 in each of the previous two years. Based on past practices, shareholders should expect a declaration by Allete on October 25, 2025 of a quarterly dividend at the existing rate of $0.73 per share, with the Record Date and Ex-Div date November 15, 2025, and Pay date December 1, 2025. If the Merger is not completed until after November 15, 2025, the Shareholders will be entitled to additional dividend payment per share per day of $0.73 divided by 91.

Opportunity For One Last Bite Of The Cherry

Let's assume the Merger is completed and payment is made to the Shareholders on December 31, 2025. Existing Shareholders have various options available: sell now at or around the October 22, 2025 closing share price of $67.34; hold through completion of the Merger; buy additional shares to hold through completion. data.title: Tesla Q3: The Storm Investors Don't See
data.description:
On October 22, 2025, Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) reported its Q3 ‘25 earnings. At first glance, the figures looked pretty decent. Revenue came in at ~$28 billion, outpacing market estimates of $26.7 billion by about 5%.

However, many, including myself, argue that this revenue beat was expected amid the EV tax credit still being available during Q3 ‘25. I believe consumers likely rushed to buy Teslas before the $7,500 federal tax credit expired in September, and as a result, boosted sales, which showed up in this quarter.

Earnings-wise, non-GAAP diluted EPS came in at $0.50, which missed market estimates of $0.56. That’s a notable miss on profitability, and this tells investors something critical about Tesla’s actual business health underneath the surface-level figures.

Then there’s the awkward sidenote about Tesla CEO Elon Musk asking for a $1 trillion compensation package - ostensibly to lock him in for the next decade or so as CEO by setting lofty milestones - as insurance he will have at least a "strong influence" over the robot army that the company is planning to build.

I’m not convinced investors are happy with how things are going.

With that, and for all the reasons outlined below, my Strong Sell rating for the company stays in place.

Q3 ‘25 Core Financials

According to its Q3 ‘25 financials, Tesla’s revenue rose 12% to ~$28 billion compared to the same period last year. The growth is driven by the overall revenue increases across all their business segments. Here’s a glance at Tela’s segmental revenue performance:

Segments

Q3 ‘24

Change (%)

$20,016
